figures.caption: Fig. 40–1. In a static fluid the force per unit area across any surface is normal to the surface and is the same for all orientations of the surface.
Fig. 40–2. The pressure in a static liquid.
Fig. 40–3. The net pressure force on a cube is −∇p-\Fignabla p per unit volume.
Fig. 40–4. The acceleration of a fluid particle.
Fig. 40–5. Streamlines in steady fluid flow.
Fig. 40–6. Fluid motion in a flow tube.
Fig. 40–8. With a re-entrant discharge tube, the stream contracts to one-half the area of the opening.
Fig. 40–9. The pressure is lowest where the velocity is highest.
Fig. 40–10. Proof that vv is not equal to √2gh\sqrt{2gh}.
Fig. 40–11. (a) Ideal fluid flow past a cylinder. (b) Circulation around a cylinder. (c) The superposition of (a) and (b).
Fig. 40–12. Water with circulation draining from a tank.
Fig. 40–13. (a) A group of vortex lines at tt; (b) the same lines at a later time t′t'.
Fig. 40–14. Making a travelling vortex ring.
Fig. 40–15. A moving vortex ring (a smoke ring). (a) The vortex lines. (b) A cross section of the ring.
